data.title: CorMedix: Strong Buy After Strong Developments And A Strategy Before Earnings
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about CorMedix Inc. (NASDAQ:CRMD) at the end of June and assigned it a Buy status. CorMedix is a small biotech company that produces DefenCath, an antimicrobial catheter lock solution for reducing the incidence of catheter-related bloodstream infections [CRBSI] in adult patients receiving chronic hemodialysis through a central venous catheter.

With the stock at ~$12.5 and a base-case Fair Value estimate of $26.2, I initiated a small position. I kept my position limited to 0.3x due to their reliance on a single product. Indeed, the potential reward is higher (perhaps much higher in time) than the potential risk. That’s why I like to have many such small biotech names in my portfolio. Even if one of them fails, on average it’s very likely to have high growth from products in incipient phases.

There were two important developments in the meantime:

  • CorMedix completed the acquisition of Melinta Therapeutics, a private commercial-stage company providing innovative therapies for acute and life-threatening illnesses.
  • FY 2025 pro forma net revenue guidance was raised from the previously announced range of $325M–$350M to at least $375M.

I am raising both my Fair Value estimate and my position slightly. I also think that it’s a good moment to buy a Call option for about 25% of my position, with the intention to sell it before the next earnings, when IV (Implied Volatility) is expected to be higher.

Business Highlights

This is what the CorMedix portfolio looks like after the Melinta acquisition:

In my initial article I analysed in detail the market and competition for DefenCath, so I will summarize:

  • Starting from ~500K hemodialysis patients in the US, with 3 treatments per week, 2 vials per treatment, and taking about 25% as potential market, we reach ~39M vials (and that’s close to the company’s estimate too).
  • Starting from a $250 list price for DefenCath per vial, I assume a price reduction after the expiration of NTAP (New Technology Add-On Payment) and TDAPA (Transitional Drug Add-On Payment), combined with some political pressure and other discounts, to $80 per vial. That gives 39M vials x $80 = $3.12B.
  • As mentioned in my initial article, there are other catheter-lock solutions on the market, other potential new entrants, and even potential new treatments in the future. With their first-mover advantage, I assume 20%-25% market share, for a total of ~$750M peak sales.
  • I will go one step further with my rather conservative approach, because there is a possibility for their new US patent expiring in 2042 to be challenged. Therefore, I will stick with $700M for 2033 and 2034.

However, the vast majority of Melinta’s drugs are antibiotics and antifungals developed and commercialized specifically for use in hospital and acute care settings, a highly competitive market with moderate growth, due to complex procedures and limited payments for hospitals. CorMedix estimates that Melinta’s revenues will grow from $120M in 2024 to $125M-$135M in 2025. Therefore, I will model a gradually slower growth from ~7% to 1% in 2034.

Let’s move to the combined pipeline:

In my initial article, I modeled $160M peak sales for DefenCath TPN, in line with $150M to $200M seen by the company. Since it’s not approved yet, I would normally ponder it with a probability factor. However, there are other possible indications for DefenCath (see Figure #2); therefore, I stick with $160M.

Regarding the potential use of Rezzayo for the prophylaxis of invasive fungal infections in adult patients undergoing allogeneic blood and marrow transplant, the company thinks that peak sales for this indication could exceed $200M. However, this time I will take a probability factor of 80% since it’s not approved yet. Then, with increased political pressure on drug prices, I will model only $120M peak sales, net of royalties. Besides, there are up to $25M milestone payments if the FDA approves the expanded indication of Rezzayo, so I assume that these will offset 2027 sales.

Financial Analysis

In a September investor presentation, CorMedix offered the following guidance for the combined revenue:

However, two days ago (October 20), the company has announced strong preliminary unaudited third-quarter financial results and a significant update to its full-year outlook, from $325M-$350M to at least $375M.

Switching to profitability, I explained that I expect gross margin to go down a little bit after TDAPA expiration to a more normal level of ~78%. Melinta also came with a healthy 77% gross margin, and I will use 77% as a terminal gross margin.

Melinta did have huge SG&A expenses, with close to zero operating margin, but CorMedix is touting some normal post-merger synergies. I do, however, decrease my 2034 EBIT margin estimate to 42.2%, especially due to higher SG&A with this diversity of products.

Finally, let’s look a bit at CorMedix’s new convertible debt that they took to fund this acquisition. The interest rate is 4%, and the conversion price is ~$13.5, which is a little OTM (Out-of-The-Money), but not by much. Therefore, I will model half of it as debt (usually modelled for deep OTM conversion prices) and half of it as equity (considering a conversion).

I use 11.55% WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital), mostly coming from 12.5% cost of equity (about 85% in the capital structure). I use this high cost of equity because CorMedix still relies more than 50% on a single product. This diversification brought by Melinta is good, but I estimated about 73% of 2034 revenue from DefenCath.

I generally rate these high-reward, high-risk stocks as “Strong Buy” if:

  • There is more than 100% potential upside to my base-case Fair Value estimate. In this case there is ~147% potential upside from yesterday’s closing price ($11.2) to Fair Value ($27.8).
  • The stock is still undervalued in a slightly pessimistic scenario, and indeed, there is ~35% potential upside in this case.
  • The company does not rely on a single product. This is still on the verge, but I already count it in Cost of equity; therefore, I will not count it here again.

A Strategy Before Earnings

If we look at IV Rank, IV (Implied Volatility) seems to be moderate for CorMedix:

However, there is a very clear pattern, with IV climbing just before earnings, especially for short-term options, and the next earnings report is expected on November 13th. IV30 (Implied Volatility for options expiring in 30 days) is about 76% now, but it was, on average, more than 108% before earnings. Maybe it won’t be like that now because they already announced some pre-earnings results; therefore, I expect somewhere around 100%.

IV60 (that means options expiring in December) is around 72% now, but here is an interesting thing: on November 13th, these options expiring in December will have only ~36 days until expiration, much closer to IV30. Therefore, I expect their IV to go to ~95%, which means about a 33% increase.

And when I find a stock seriously undervalued and I expect IV to go up, I sometimes buy an ATM (At-The-Money) Call option, with the intention to sell it just before earnings, when IV will be higher. This is why I bought in the last two days some 12 Call Dec’25 options, for an average of $106. Let's see how they would perform just before earnings with that 33% expected increase in IV:

We can observe that break-even is slightly lower at $10.8-$10.9. Despite the normal time decay for any short-term long options, the higher IV should more than offset that.

An alternative is to buy a Call option expiring March ’26, about 5 months from now, for an average bid-ask premium of ~$213. In this case, time decay will be much slower, but also the jump in the IV just before earnings will be much less pronounced (I expect 5% to 10%). ROI should be lower for increasing stock prices, due to a higher cost, but also the loss should be lower for decreasing stock prices for the same reason:

Table 3: CRMD 12 Call Dec'25 vs. Mar'26 (Author)

These are just estimates, because nobody can guarantee the IV for the two options just before earnings.

The December expiration is much more compelling on the upside, break-even is very slightly better, but the March expiration is a little better on the downside, so I will also try to buy a March one if the premium goes down this week. On the downside, it will perform slightly worse, so here is how I plan to continue:

  • If the Call options will be clearly profitable: just close them.
  • If the Call options will be around break-even: I will very likely switch to selling options, since the premium is higher just before earnings. Maybe a naked Put option (if I will find the premium compelling) or, if not, a Put Spread.
  • If the Call options will be at a loss: I will very likely buy more shares, with CorMedix around $10 or lower, it should be even more undervalued.

Position and Risk Management

In my slightly pessimistic scenario, I already modeled that their pipeline is doomed, for both DefenCath and Rezzayo, and I still found the stock slightly undervalued.

However, I also talked about the major risk of relying on a single product for more than 50% of their business. If something bad were to happen, it could be catastrophic for CorMedix. And even if not some serious quality issues, sometimes a generic drugmaker can attack their patent, take a look at what happened to ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY) after the Indian drugmaker Lupin challenged their neffy patents. Even if this might not be an event with high chances of 100% success, it might take 2-3 years to settle it.

That’s why I keep my CorMedix position limited. As mentioned in my initial article, I had about 0.3x exposure through shares, and I intend to add up to 0.1x now with these Call options, for a total exposure of no more than 0.4x.

Like mentioned in the introduction, I have about 10 biotech companies in my portfolio (through shares and/or options), with limited positions for each, so even if one will completely fail, I am still well covered. A question could be: then, why not a single big pharma with more than 10 products? Of course, managing 10 positions takes significantly more time, but the return is, on average, significantly higher, because I target small companies in incipient phases (just launched products, or with high chances of approval) with very high growth rates. And I still didn’t find the big company with all their 10 products in this phase.

Takeaway

CorMedix's acquisition of Melinta Therapeutics diversifies its portfolio and reduces single-product risk. With strong performance for DefenCath in CRBSI, FY 2025 pro forma net revenue guidance was raised from the previously announced range of $325M–$350M to at least $375M.

DefenCath CRBSI remains the primary revenue driver, but additional growth could come from Melinta's hospital-focused drugs and, especially, from their pipeline opportunities for DefenCath and Rezzayo.

Derived from a 10-year DCF model, my base-case fair value estimate is $27.8, offering ~147% upside from current levels (~$11.2). In a slightly pessimistic scenario, with their pipeline doomed, and reduced peak sales for DefenCath CRBSI, the stock still looks slightly undervalued, with a fair value estimate of $15.2.

I am slightly increasing my position:

  • I keep my ~0.3x longer-term stock position. I keep the position limited due to their reliance on a single product, and I prefer a broad portfolio of ~10 small risky biotech positions like this, but with strong growth expected ahead.
  • I add up to 0.1x through Call options, with the intention to close them just before earnings, when IV and options premium are higher. In the event that my Call options will be at a loss before earnings due to stock price depreciation, I still target ~0.4x exposure either by buying more shares, or by selling Put options / Put spreads.

data.title: Hormel Foods: Shares Haven't Been This Cheap In A Decade
data.description:
Hormel Foods (NYSE:HRL) shares have performed poorly lately as lackluster results and general consumer staple weakness have hit the stock hard. But this analyst believes problems are only temporary, with investors glossing over qualities that make the company an attractive choice today.

Introduction

Hormel Foods is one of the world's largest packaged food companies with a specialty in protein-rich products.

The company got its start in Austin, Minnesota, back in 1891. It began as a small pork processor that purchased animals from local farms, emphasizing quality and innovation. Founder George Hormel chose pork processing because it offered greater profit potential versus other animals, since every part of the pig was used in some way.

Hormel introduced the nation's first canned ham in 1927, and eventually followed that up with other products like Dinty Moore Stew and Hormel Chili. In 1937 it officially started selling Spam, and the canned meat product was an instant hit. Spam remains Hormel's marquee product even into the 21st century.

These days, Hormel is a diversified food giant. It is a leader in 40+ retail categories, owning brands such as the aforementioned Spam, but also Planters nuts, Skippy peanut butter, and Wholly guacamole. It also has a joint venture with Mexican foods giant Herdez. Here's a snapshot of some of the newer products put out by some of the various brands under the Hormel umbrella, taken from the 2024 annual report.

Hormel doesn't just own brands that you know from the grocery store, either. The company has a robust foodservice division, which includes products like prepared proteins, turkey, bacon, and pizza toppings. It also sells salty snacks like nuts to other food manufacturers, who then process these nuts into other products.

Finally, Hormel also has an international division as it exports various products to other markets. Your author was just in South Korea during the country's Chuseok holiday, and every grocery store had large Spam gift set displays for the occasion. Spam was introduced to South Korea by U.S. soldiers during the Korean War, and it has become a tradition to give gift sets to both family and friends during the holiday. Plus, Spam is both salty and high in fat, which mixes well with Korean food that's generally spicy and tangy.

Put it all together, and Hormel does approximately $12B per year in revenue, with retail accounting for 62% of sales, foodservice generating 32% of sales, and the international division adding about 6% to the top line.

As we all collectively get older and eating habits change, Hormel is a natural beneficiary. Nutrition science is now urging us to eat more protein and fewer carbs as the former keeps us full longer. Hormel's portfolio is significantly higher in protein than most of its peers, and even most of its non-meat items are still protein-rich. This should provide a long-term tailwind.

And yet, despite this advantage, Hormel shares are struggling. The stock recently tanked following a weak earnings report and similarly tepid short-term outlook, with shares falling to a new 10-year low. A decade ago the stock traded at $33 each; these days, shares trade hands for just over $24 each.

Let's take a closer look at Hormel today, starting with the issues that are impacting the share price and whether these are permanent or just temporary headwinds.

What happened?

Hormel came out with Q3 earnings on August 28th, which came in under analyst expectations.

Although volume was relatively strong -- sales increased by 4.5% in the quarter and beat analyst expectations -- the bottom line was much worse. Adjusted profits were $0.35 per share, which missed analyst expectations by $0.06 per share.

More importantly, the company slashed its outlook for the rest of the year. It originally guided for adjusted earnings of between $1.58 and $1.68 per share, but ratcheted down expectations to between $1.43 and $1.45 per share.

The big culprit has been input costs. The company highlighted increased costs of pork, beef, and nuts as the primary factor hurting near-term profitability. For instance, the price of pork bellies were up approximately 30%. Beef prices also remain elevated, as your local grocer's meat section will confirm.

Put it all together, and these issues raised costs by approximately 400 basis points.

While Hormel waits for commodity input costs to fall to a more sustainable level, it does plan to pass through certain price increases to customers. But this is a process that takes a little while, and customers are already weary after big inflation-related price increases in 2022 and 2023.

Investors were disappointed by the numbers, but mostly sent shares lower on the tepid outlook. The company was also mum about 2026, but it's pretty clear investors expect more of the same next year, too.

Despite this poor near-term outlook, I believe the long-term still looks pretty good for Hormel. Let's zoom out a little bit and look at more of the big picture.

Long-term outlook

Bears like to point to Hormel's lack of long-term EPS growth as a bearish signal, and I'm the first to admit they have a point.

If earnings come in the $1.33 to $1.35 per share range as management expects, there will be pretty much zero growth in the bottom line from 2017 through 2025 -- on a per share basis, anyway.

Analysts remain bullish over the longer-term, with expectations for earnings growth to resume in 2026 and continue for the next few years after that. According to consensus estimates, EPS will improve to $1.54 per share in 2026 and steadily increase to $1.92 per share in 2029.

The company is also bullish, telling investors that it projects long-term sales to increase by about 2-3% per year, with operating income increasing by 5-7% annually.

There are a few factors that should combine to give Hormel enough oomph to reach these growth targets.

The first is Hormel's position in the marketplace. Remember, this is a company that is either the leader or the number two in more than 40 different retail categories. This position does give it a certain amount of pricing power, and retail leaders tend to stay atop their category. Spam, Hormel Chili, or Planters nuts aren't about to be overtaken by some sexy new technology.

Stodgy companies like Hormel can also use tech to make operations more efficient. The company is in the middle of its Transform and Modernize initiative, which will close certain facilities and use modern tools to make the whole operation run more efficiently. These cost savings will flow straight down to the bottom line.

Hormel should also get relief from higher input costs. Investors might remember high cocoa costs impacting Hershey shares earlier this year. While the price of cocoa is still elevated versus a few years ago, the commodity has fallen from a 52-week high of more than $12,000 per tonne to closer to $6,000 per tonne. High commodity prices often work themselves out as more producers enter the market.

As input costs drop, Hormel's margins should improve to more historical levels. Over the last 12 months, Hormel has posted 16.4% gross margins. That's the company's lowest gross margin in a decade, and is far removed from gross margins in the 19-22% range it was able to deliver for most of the past decade.

The company also has a solid balance sheet, which is always a bonus. Its trailing debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 1.3x, which is much better than peers like PepsiCo, General Mills, or Campbell's. Low debt levels give a company relief during tough times, with greater flexibility to explore an acquisition should such an opportunity present itself.

Put it all together, and I believe that Hormel's results will normalize. That will lead to EPS growth that surpasses sales growth, with results steadily improving over the next few years as costs come down.

And, as we'll see, Hormel shares are currently available for a bargain price. This means we could potentially see some multiple expansion to help fuel returns.

Valuation

For the better part of the last decade, Hormel shares traded at a premium valuation as investors put emphasis on the company's high quality portfolio, its strong financial position, and its enviable 50+ year dividend growth streak.

That isn't the case today. Hormel shares are currently trading for about 16x forward earnings, which is the company's cheapest valuation in a decade. It's also significantly under the mean valuation in the last 10 years, which is right around 22x forward earnings.

We'll also note that the company has traded as high as 31x forward earnings, which was back in 2020.

Hormel is also cheap on a dividend yield basis. I follow a simple rule here: if a company's dividend yield is much higher than its long-term average, it often indicates a stock is undervalued. The opposite is likely true if the current dividend yield is much lower than average.

Hormel's dividend yield is currently in the 4.8% range, which is the company's highest yield in a decade.

We'll note that this chart uses Hormel's forward dividend yield rather than the trailing one, and it assumes that the dividend will be hiked again in 2026.

Hormel is trading for a valuation that implies there won't be much growth going forward. I disagree with that assessment, as do analysts and the company's own outlook. A decade-low valuation combined with some growth should be enough to propel shares higher -- and investors get a fantastic dividend while they wait.

Risks

The big risk here is input costs remaining elevated. I believe they will normalize over time, but commodities are unpredictable. Various ailments could do major damage to cow, pig, chicken, or turkey populations in the U.S., which would both drive up prices and make supply harder to obtain.

The other risk is investor sentiment surrounding the consumer staples sector continuing to erode. Hormel enjoyed a premium valuation a few years ago partly because investors were bullish on consumer staples. That attitude has changed, and that's part of the reason why shares have struggled. Further bearishness could hit the stock even harder.

The bottom line

The last couple of years haven't been great for Hormel. Input costs are increasing, and the company hasn't been able to push through price increases quickly enough to offset that impact. This has hit the bottom line, and these pressures are expected to persist for a little while longer.

But the longer-term picture doesn't look so bad. Input costs should improve, which should drive margins higher. The company's brand portfolio should have attractive tailwinds as many North Americans choose to eat more protein. The balance sheet is great, and the cost-cutting initiative should help grow the bottom line.

If you believe -- like this analyst does -- that Hormel will return to growth again, then buying shares at a decade-low valuation and decade-high dividend yield is an attractive proposition. I rate the stock a buy, especially for long-term dividend investors.

data.title: Washington's Quantum Push Puts Rigetti In Focus
data.description:
Quantum stocks have taken a sharp sell-off over recent weeks, with most investors freaking out as valuations return to reality after massive gains. When I initially reviewed Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) back in April, it remained a bet on quantum computing's future. Since then, the stock has climbed approximately 335%, supported by actual technology advances.

Report that the Trump administration intends for the Commerce Department to invest in quantum firms indicates broadening government involvement with key technologies. This move further establishes Rigetti as a strategically significant player as a relatively rare quantum hardware player with a likely shot at investment and greater government collaboration. What was a small-cap science project in the past now threatens to emerge as a serious player in the quest for commercial quantum lead.

Rigetti's Q3 Results Could Mark a Turning Point

Rigetti will announce third-quarter 2025 results in November, for which analysts estimate $2.17 million in revenue as well as EPS of negative $0.04, a 55% year-over-year improvement in loss per share. While the business continues operating in the investment phase, the market cares more about execution milestones than near-term profitability. Analysts over the past three-month period revised EPS upward three times compared to a single revision downward, indicating increased confidence in Rigetti’s operating advances after having received new system orders as well as expansion of its Novera platform.

Consensus revenue changes indicate a more cautious tone, with one upward revision and three downtowns, more a function of timing changes in hardware deliveries than declining demand. Two EPS beats out of six misses for Rigetti underscore volatility characteristic of early-stage tech companies, but increasing predictability from management has already altered sentiment. Investors will look for news about progress on its 100-plus-qubit Cepheus system, assurance that its partnership with Taiwan's Quanta Computer remains on track, as well as discussion of government grants associated with the US National Quantum Initiative.

For perspective, Rigetti reported Q2 revenue of $1.8 million with a 31% gross margin, which was less than 64% during the prior-year period, a result of a contract mix. Q3 will determine if cost discipline holds as R&D expenses stage milestones ahead. With over $570 million in liquidity, Rigetti has the financial statement strength to withstand losses as it drives towards commercialization.

In the long run, revenues will rise as demand for governments' and enterprises' increases. Its bigger picture is a transition from sporadic system sales to recurring Quantum-Computing-as-a-Service (QCaaS) revenue, more acquisitions-chargeable, subscription-esque revenue that's tied to access to cloud as well as software integration.

Rigetti business has two pillars, on-prem selling hardware, and cloud access to quantum. Both are yet small but scalable once commercial demand builds out. Most recent revenue derives from government and research contracts, which may swing with federal budgets. Temporal expiration of the US National Quantum Initiative depressed results, but its planned renewal later this year will get activity.

Hardware sales are logging early traction. Rigetti announced last month $5.7 million in system orders, including a large Asian technology firm and a California AI startup order. They are indicative of increasing overseas interest, although margins are low for now.

Access via Rigetti’s Quantum Cloud Services (QCS) platform offers higher margins and recurring opportunities. As more integration with Microsoft Azure and other hyperscalers occurs, QCS may turn into a predictable subscription-style revenue stream like AWS’s compute businesses.

The Fundamental Advantage: Chiplet Architecture

Its greatest strength lies in its chiplet-based superconducting architecture, which is the only modular multi-chip quantum system in production commerce. In connecting several small chips rather than a large chip, Rigetti will be able to increase yields, decrease manufacturing risk, and scale performance more rapidly. Rigetti’s superconducting technology is built for speed and scalability, giving it a real chance to reach commercial quantum advantage before competitors, turning cutting-edge research into practical computing power and long-term revenue growth.

The roadmap is certain: this 36-qubit machine will yield a 100-or-more-qubit machine by the fourth quarter of 2025, with 300- and 1,000-qubit machines, representing a progression towards increased accuracy (99.9% fidelity) and increased gate speed under 50 nanoseconds, both being crucial thresholds for quantum advantage.

Compared with competing technologies based on trapped-ion or neutral-atom technologies, superconducting qubits at Rigetti are more rapid and rapid to produce with existing semiconductor tools. This will make it easy to infuse them into standard data centers, a crucial step towards eventual commercial application.

Today, the Cepheus-1-36Q operates with almost flawless precision, demonstrating its new multi-chip architecture to be stable as well as scalable. This is significant as it gets Rigetti further towards developing larger, more capable quantum systems that might eventually surpass older-style computers, bringing decades of research to tangible commercial reality.

Valuing the Quantum Frontier

Traditional valuation metrics are not relevant to Rigetti, which remains in the early stages of commercialization with modest revenue but high CapEx. In general, traditional metrics like P/E or EV/EBITDA are not relevant, for the whole sector. Investors judge development by execution milestones as well as by technological differentiation.

Rigetti's price is 1,583x its EV/Sales, much greater than IonQ's 385x or D-Wave's 459x, primarily since its revenue base is small but its future growth expectations are large. Its multiple of 1,259x Price/Sales shows comparable bullishness about its long-term monetization that its chiplet architecture as well as its future 100-qubit Cepheus system will propel.

Its Price-to-Book of 23.4x also outranks IonQ (13.8x) and D-Wave (15.8x), which reflects a premium for its proprietary US-located IP and its Fab-1 factory. These lofty multiples resemble the early valuations of AI hardware companies that appeared expensive initially but eventually grew into their valuations as revenue scaled.

All that matters now for Rigetti’s valuation, regardless of its price, is demonstrating commercial traction in the form of system deliveries, cloud revenue accelerations, and IP-located government-backed partnerships that turn such engineering milestones into recurring cash flow.

Challenges on the Way to Quantum Advantage

Rigetti’s greatest risk is execution. The quantum advantage roadmap of its parent, scaled to 1,000 qubits with 99.9% fidelity, remains aggressive and prone to technical risk. All it will take is a slowdown in hardware speed, chiplet yield, or error correction to move commercialization further into the future.

Strong reliance on government initiatives such as the US National Quantum Initiative and the UK’s NQCC also opens it to policy as well as budget cycles. As it has more than $570 million in cash, its high R&D intensity implies steady cash burn until revenue scales. Competition remains a worry, with bigger players such as IBM, Google, and IonQ improving fast.

Lastly, running discussions related to hoped-for US government equity participation may bolster investment but add control or restrictions that bind strategic flexibility. All in all, long-term opportunity at Rigetti remains large, but development needs to stay steady in order to warrant its high valuation as well as maintain investor confidence.

Bottom Line

Ever since my initial April analysis, it appears that Rigetti came out of pure speculation. With a revolutionary architecture, powerful partnerships, and more than half a billion in cash, it now resides at the edge of viable quantum computing.

For investors, the Trump administration’s plan for the Commerce Department to take equity stakes in quantum companies marks a potential turning point where quantum stocks like Rigetti could shift from speculative bets to strategically backed national assets with stronger long-term support and credibility.

data.title: Super Micro Computer Continues To Overpromise And Underdeliver - Sell
data.description:
I have covered Super Micro Computer, Inc. or "Super Micro" (NASDAQ:SMCI) (NEOE:SMCI:CA) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

With the AI data center hype raging at full force again, Super Micro Computer's shares have been mostly riding the coattails of poster child NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) in recent quarters:

While the company's stock price has doubled recent lows, financial results continue to disappoint.

After missing Q1/FY2025 sales expectations and issuing weak Q2/FY2025 guidance in November 2024, Super Micro warned of poor Q3/FY2025 results in April with revenues falling well short of consensus expectations at that time.

Three months ago, the company reported weaker-than-expected Q4/FY2025 results and lowered FY2026 sales expectations from $40 billion to $33 billion.

While the disappointing results and outlook resulted in a 30%+ stock price decline, the ongoing AI data center hype resulted in Super Micro Computer's shares recovering most of these losses in recent weeks:

On Thursday, Super Micro Computer preannounced Q1/FY2026 sales:

On a year-over-year basis, revenues are expected to be down by approximately 20%.

According to the press release, the massive shortfall was due to "design win upgrades" which resulted in some sales being pushed into the second quarter.

Consequently, management reiterated FY2026 sales expectations of at least $33 billion:

Supermicro is seeing outstanding levels of customer engagements for newly released AI liquid cooled solutions along with numerous key customers ramping large, multi-quarter, volume deployments (...).

We see customer demand accelerating, and we are gaining AI share, reiterating revenue of at least $33B for FY 2026 with the expectation of delivering more.

Management also celebrated recent design wins "in excess of $12b" and promised near-term updates on expected Q2 deliveries and sales.

The company will report detailed Q1/FY2026 results on November 4.

Please note that in contrast to past business updates, the company abstained from providing preliminary earnings per share numbers, which isn't exactly suited to instill confidence in Super Micro's already muted margin expectations.

Assuming gross margins of around 9%, non-GAAP earnings per share would come in below $0.20, more than 50% below consensus expectations. Should gross margins drop below 6%, the company would likely report a loss.

In recent quarters, management has blamed poor results on a number of issues:

  • tariffs
  • temporary capital constraints following the surprise resignation of the company's auditor last year
  • lack of availability of Nvidia's latest generation GPUs
  • specification changes from a "major new customer"

But with most of these issues having been resolved in recent months and larger competitors like Dell (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprises (HPE) reporting strong growth in AI server sales, Super Micro Computer's persistent underperformance is concerning.

At least in my opinion, declining sales and margin pressure could be a sign of market share losses and things appear to have gotten worse in recent months.

Given Super Micro's recent pattern of overpromising and underdelivering, I find it increasingly difficult to buy into management's narrative.

Similar to FY2025, I would expect the company to lower full-year guidance further over the next couple of quarters.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Super Micro Computer's shares.

Risk Factors

As usual, the bear thesis is not without risks, particularly not in today's hype-driven market. Should the company indeed make up for the Q1 shortfall in the second quarter and deliver on full-year sales expectations without additional margin pressure, the stock might rally to new recovery highs.

Bottom Line

FY2026 is off to a very slow start for Super Micro Computer, with sales expected to decrease by approximately 20% on a year-over-year basis.

Adding insult to injury, the company abstained from providing preliminary earnings per share numbers, which does not bode well for Super Micro's margin performance.

While management considered the large miss more of a timing issue and reiterated full-year revenue guidance, the constant pattern of overpromising and underdelivering is concerning.

At least in my opinion, declining sales and margin pressure are a clear sign of market share losses.

As a result, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on Super Micro Computer's shares.

data.title: Talen Energy's Data Center Initiatives Are A Smart Investment
data.description:
Investors interested in Vistra (VST), a utility operating in the deregulated wholesale markets in the mid-Atlantic region (PJM market) and Texas (ERCOT), may also have an interest in Talen Energy (NASDAQ:TLN). I previously wrote about Vistra and discussed the mechanics of deregulated electrical utility markets in a September 28, 2025 article. I recommend reading that article to understand some of the mechanics of Talen's business, as it shares some of the same investment theses as Vistra.

Let me briefly discuss how unregulated electrical markets work. A deregulated market consists of a wholesale electrical market, where power producers like Vistra or Talen sell electricity to retail energy providers (REPs), utilities, and electricity traders. The REPs, or local utilities, then sell that electricity in the retail market directly to residential and commercial customers. In many cases, customers have a choice of local utilities or REPs in the retail market. Different utilities or REPs offer various pricing plans and energy sources, including renewable energy.

Talen operates solely on the wholesale electrical market. In contrast, Vistra sells electricity both on the wholesale market and directly to residential and commercial customers in the retail market through its subsidiaries, making it an integrated provider.

Vistra has a much larger scale than Talen, with a $68.22 billion market cap compared to Talen's $18.57 billion (as of October 17).

Investors who have a more conservative or value investor mindset would likely choose Vistra due to its larger scale and its more stable cash flow streams derived from its retail business, which provides a natural hedge to fluctuating wholesale prices (when the profitability of the wholesale business declines, the profitability of the retail business goes up).

Growth investors would likely be more interested in Talen's projected 35%+ growth in Adjusted free cash flow ("FCF") per share through 2028 and potential for additional upside.

Adjusted FCF (AFCF) is a non-GAAP measure that Talen uses to highlight the cash flow available from its core business operations by excluding one-time or non-recurring items. Some investors prefer this metric over Net Income or earnings-per-share ("EPS") since FCF is harder to manipulate in accounting than net income, making it a more reliable indicator of a company's true financial health and ability to generate value. The company emphasizes "per share" to highlight that adjusted FCF is not only growing but also growing faster than the number of shares outstanding, thereby emphasizing that the company is increasing value for existing investors.

A sustained growth rate of over 35% is exceptional. It suggests the company is in an accelerated growth phase, which is exactly what a growth investor is looking for. It implies a high return on invested capital and a strong competitive position. Momentum investors have noticed the company's robust fundamentals and have pushed the stock price much higher year to date.

Additionally, some investors believe the company has significant upside from contracting Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) with data centers. A PPA is a long-term contract between Talen and data centers to buy electricity at a pre-negotiated rate. Experts, such as the International Energy Agency (IEA), project a massive increase in electrical demand from data centers. IEA states on its website:

In the United States, power consumption by data centres is on course to account for almost half of the growth in electricity demand between now and 2030. Driven by AI [artificial intelligence] use, the U.S. economy is set to consume more electricity in 2030 for processing data than for manufacturing all energy-intensive goods combined, including aluminium, steel, cement and chemicals.

As a result, providing electricity to data centers is a growth area for several utilities and a significant thesis for investing in Talen, something that attracts thematic investors interested in growth from AI.

A first mover in contracting power to AI hyperscaler data centers

Although Talen is not the first to contract out its electrical power to a data center, some consider it a first mover by executing a significant deal to build a massive, co-located data center campus powered by a nuclear plant in March 2024. A press release briefly discussing its agreement with AWS stated:

We are expert first-movers in powering data centers with a track record of bringing our vision to life. When we developed the world's first 24x7 carbon-free, co-located data center campus powered by our Susquehanna nuclear facility, we unlocked a unique opportunity for growth by serving power demand for data centers. In March of 2024, we executed the first-of-its-kind data center transaction with Amazon (AMZN) to power its Amazon Web Services (AWS) data center campus adjacent to Susquehanna. In June of 2025, we expanded that relationship to supply electricity to Amazon using the grid for operations that support AI and cloud technologies at the data center campus with the ability to deliver to other sites throughout Pennsylvania. We have the know-how to coordinate both direct-connect "behind-the-meter" and grid-connected "front-of-the-meter" co-located load arrangements to power AI safely and reliably.

When you hear management discussing this deal, you may hear the terms "behind-the-meter" ("BTM") and "front-of-the-meter" ("FTM").

A BTM connection directly supplies power without using the primary transmission grid, which saves the customer the costs of using the primary electrical grid, including the fees and tariffs associated with building, maintaining, and using the local grid. The customer also has the option to pair a BTM system with energy storage, allowing it to regulate voltage and frequency to optimum values. A BTM system with backup batteries also has other technical advantages that are desirable.

An FTM model is a scenario in which a power supplier (often renewable sources) supplies electricity directly to the utility grid for the benefit of a group of customers. A BTM system provides more reliable power than a shared grid (FTM) model, which is subject to congestion, weather-related outages, and age-related failures. Last, A direct BTM connection can avoid lengthy regulatory approvals, grid stability studies, and major utility construction projects that FTM implementations require, which allows the power provider and customer to bring the direct connection online faster. However, Talen encountered issues with its BTM implementation involving one regulator. So, BTM doesn't evade all regulation.

Given all the advantages of the BTM setup, aside from pushback from regulators, you may be wondering why customers like Amazon also utilize an FTM setup. The problem with BTM is that it has constraints on the amount of power it can deliver. The primary reason customers choose an FTM setup is its access to the entire grid and significantly more scale to power a data center. For instance, a typical BTM generator's capacity (MWs) is dwarfed by the utility grid's capacity (GWs).

Additionally, some view the FTM model as more reliable. Since the electrical grid has many power sources connected, the loss of one generating source won't cause a total power loss because electrical grid engineers can bring other power sources online. In contrast, a single BTM generator failure directly cuts power to the data center facility.

Talen and AWS's initial connection was a BTM arrangement that connected 300 MW of power from Talen's Susquehanna nuclear power plant to the data center directly, allowing AWS to secure dedicated, fixed-price, carbon-free power quickly, avoiding the lengthy interconnection process and other costs associated with relying entirely on the public grid.

However, Talen Energy and AWS signed a new 1,920-MW PPA in June 2025. Under the new PPA, the existing 300-MW co-location arrangement will change to an FTM framework that doesn't require Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) approval for some wholesale activities and participation in interstate energy markets. Although a BTM arrangement can theoretically avoid FERC market rules, it is not without its limitations. The Talen-AWS BTM arrangement encountered opposition from FERC because the regulator feared that such a significant power source (1,920 megawatts) sending power to only one consumer (AWS) might adversely impact the stability of the regional electrical grid. Therefore, Talen had to change its PPA deal with Amazon from a BTM deal to an FTM deal. The press release announcing this new PPA stated:

Under the terms of a new power purchase agreement ("PPA"), Talen will supply electricity to Amazon for operations that support AI and other cloud technologies at Amazon's data center campus adjacent to Susquehanna, with the ability to deliver to other sites throughout Pennsylvania. Talen and Amazon will also explore building new Small Modular Reactors ("SMRs") within Talen's Pennsylvania footprint and pursue expanding the nuclear plant's energy output through uprates, with the intent to add net-new energy to the PJM grid. Under the expanded PPA, at the full contract quantity, Talen will provide Amazon with 1,920 megawatts of carbon-free nuclear power through 2042, with options to further extend its duration. The power delivery schedule will ramp over time, expecting to achieve the full volume no later than 2032, with the potential to meaningfully accelerate. This long-term transaction will significantly decrease Talen's market risk and minimize its reliance on the Federal nuclear production tax credit.

Talen's new PPA agreement with AWS offers multiple benefits for the utility, making it more attractive to investors.

The agreement helps Talen to become less reliant on the Federal nuclear production tax credit ("PTC"), which subsidizes nuclear power production. The problem with becoming overly dependent upon a government-backed subsidy is that it introduces the risk that the government could yank those funds away due to political considerations. The Amazon PPA agreement locks in a revenue stream for up to 20 years, providing the power company with revenue visibility and greater certainty about its future, which reliance on PTC subsidies cannot offer. The company's PPA contracted cash flows give it greater balance sheet flexibility than revenue dependent on short-term or politically variable tax credits. For just this reason alone, the market will award the company a premium valuation for that revenue visibility and certainty.

The new AWS PPA is a significant factor in accelerating Talen's Adjusted Free Cash Flow per share growth, which investors use to value the company. Part of the new PPA eliminates a one-time option to cap AWS power commitments at 480 MW under the original data center arrangement. Instead, Talen secured a higher, accelerated commitment from Amazon, which contributes to higher projected FCF growth. The company expects the new PPA agreement to contribute approximately 10% of the overall total FCF/share projected growth by 2028 and up to 20% by 2032.

Management also expects the new PPA agreement to serve as a platform for future technology collaboration between Talen and AWS to pursue nuclear uprates (increasing the power output of the existing plant) and Small Modular Reactor ("SMR") development around Talen's footprint, positioning the company for future energy technology growth.

Valuation

Talen's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 5.33, much higher than the utility P/S median of 2.46 and higher than the utility peers that I compare it to on the following chart, suggesting that the market either overvalues the company or investors expect revenue to increase rapidly in the future or that its revenue will become much more valuable (higher margins).

However, the market generally prefers valuing utilities using metrics like Enterprise Value ("EV")/EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization), price-to-earnings (P/E), and FCF, rather than the P/S ratio.

The following chart shows Talen's EBITDA growth from analysts' estimated $1.0131 billion in 2025 at a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 45.82% to reach $2.192 billion in 2027.

The following table compares Talen Energy's Forward EV/EBITDA, EBITDA CAGR to 2027, and TTM EBITDA to several of its utility peers.

Company Name Forward EV-to-EBITDA EBITDA CAGR to 2027 TTM EBITDA Margin
Constellation Energy (CEG) 25.23 13.54% 25.62%
Talen Energy 20.85 45.82% 54.01%
The AES Corp (AES) 16.37 10.37% 25.72%
Vistra Corp 14.90 13.23% 47.13%
American Electrical Power (AEP) 12.26 10.27% 41.79%
Exelon Corp (EXC) 11.03 7.41% 35.27%
NRG Energy (NRG) 10.79 10.21% 12.46%

Source for EV/EBITDA, Two-year EBITDA CAGR, and TTM EBITDA margin: YCharts.

The market generally considers the company with the highest EV/EBITDA as overvalued when compared with companies with lower EV/EBITDA values. Since Talen has the second-highest Forward EV-to-EBITDA of 20.85, some may consider it overvalued compared to all but Constellation Energy (EV/EBITDA 25.23). However, Talen's superior margins (54.01%) and EBITDA growth rates (45.82%) may justify its higher EV/EBITDA valuation. Based on the above comparison, the market may consider Talen Energy fairly valued to slightly undervalued.

The company's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) is 106.88, well above the utilities sector median of 21.41, which indicates either that the market overvalues the stock or that the market expects earnings to proliferate in the future.

Talen's forward 2025 P/E to analysts' estimated growth rate ("PEG") ratio is 0.28, calculated by dividing its 2025 forward P/E of 74.65 by its estimated EPS growth rate of 264.46%. Generally, the market considers PEG ratios below 1.0 as a sign of undervaluation. Suppose it sold at a 2025 PEG ratio of 1.0; the stock price would be $1,438.66, approximately 254% above its October 17 market close of $406.45.

However, the market is unlikely to award the stock a PEG ratio of 1.0 because it likely doesn't believe the company can sustain such high EPS growth. Analysts' forecast of EPS growth decelerating to 28.77% in 2027 indicates the consensus among analysts that Talen's EPS growth rate will eventually decline. The company's 2025 PEG ratio may still have upside, though, because momentum investors are likely to be enticed by rapid EPS growth over the next two years, but investors shouldn't expect it to reach a PEG ratio of 1.0.

Normally, I would do a reverse discount cash flow ("DCF") using the standard calculation for FCF. However, although Talen has a positive TTM adjusted FCF, its standard TTM FCF is negative $160 million. I decided to do a DCF using its TTM adjusted FCF, which equals its second quarter 2025 adjusted FCF of -$78 million + its first quarter 2025 adjusted FCF of $87 million + its fourth quarter 2025 adjusted FCF of $21 million + its third quarter 2025 adjusted FCF of $97 million = TTM adjusted FCF of $127 million.

The following reverse discounted cash flow ("DCF") uses a terminal growth rate of 3%, reflecting what I believe will be the U.S. market's long-term inflation rate. This reverse DCF uses a discount rate of 10%, which I base on Talen's weighted average cost of capital of 9.84%, indicating a moderate risk level.

Reverse DCF

The second quarter of fiscal year ("FY") 2026 reported TTM FCF (in millions)

 $127
Terminal growth rate 3%
Discount Rate 10%
Years 1-10 growth rate 34.7%
Stock Price (October 17, 2025, closing price) $406.45
Terminal FCF value $2.572 billion
Discounted Terminal Value $14.167 billion
TTM FCF margin ** 5.9%

** I calculated the FCF margin using TTM operating revenue excluding Unrealized gain (loss) on derivative instruments, which equals $2,128 million. Several analysts exclude the gain (loss) on derivatives to avoid the volatility of the mark-to-market adjustments on these derivatives.

Talen's official financial statements use GAAP "Operating Revenues" that include "Unrealized gain (loss) on derivative instruments." Investors should be aware that some websites show the company's total second quarter 2025 revenue as $630 million, and other websites show the company's total second quarter 2025 revenue as $454 million, which excludes Unrealized gain (loss) on derivative instruments.

The reverse DCF indicates that Talen would need to grow adjusted FCF at a 34.7% annual rate over the next ten years. This scenario is improbable for a capital-intensive utility/power producer. Although the company forecasts adjusted FCF annual growth of 35% to 40% up until 2028, growth at that rate is unlikely to last because it relies on several significant drivers, such as the AWS PPA and accretive M&A (mergers and acquisitions), that may be challenging to replicate. Additionally, Talen will likely run into the law of large numbers over the next ten years, which means as a company's FCF and asset base grow, it will become exponentially more challenging to find and execute new projects (such as new Data Center PPAs, PPA expansions, or acquisitions) large enough to maintain above 30% adjusted FCF growth.

Although this reverse DCF suggests the market may overvalue Talen in the long term (10 years), momentum growth investors are likely more interested in the high adjusted FCF growth over the next two to three years. They will likely ignore reverse DCF or DCF valuations over ten years or even five years.

Risks

Some investors have significant concerns about the company's balance sheet and leverage risk. Talen has $122 million in cash and cash equivalents versus $2.97 billion in long-term debt. Its debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio is 2.47, indicating that the company finances its operations more with debt than with shareholder equity. For a capital-intensive industry like utilities, the market may find that a D/E ratio of 2.47 is acceptable.

The company has an interest coverage of 0.53, which may deter some conservative investors because it indicates the company is not generating sufficient earnings to cover the interest on its debts. If the company's operating earnings ever drop, it could struggle to meet debt obligations.

Talen's debt-to-EBITDA is 5.79, calculated by dividing the total debt of $3.072 billion by the company's TTM EBITDA of $531 million. Although this ratio appears high, it may be a deceptive metric for the company at this time because it may include recent debt from two large acquisitions, but does not yet reflect the full EBITDA that the purchase will generate. The company expects the acquisition of the natural gas Freedom and Guernsey plants to be accretive to FCF/share by more than 40% in 2026 and more than 50% in 2027-2029. Talen raised a $1.6 billion credit facility on October 16, 2025. The following image from the company's September 2025 Investor Presentation presents the debt situation in more detail.

The company has stated in a February 27 2025 8-K, "We are focused on maintaining net leverage below our target of 3.5x net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA, along with ample liquidity." Investors should monitor the company's progress toward its net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA target and follow its bond ratings. As of July 18, 2025, Fitch gave Talen a Long-Term Issuer Default Rating ("IDR") of BB-, which is a non-investment-grade speculative rating.

As an independent power producer (IPP), Talen has traditionally relied on highly volatile wholesale power prices. This volatility makes the cash flow needed to service its high debt load less predictable, increasing the risk for creditors and shareholders, which is where the PPA agreement with AWS comes in as a derisking factor by converting a large portion of the Susquehanna nuclear plant's capacity from volatile merchant power sales (selling electricity at wholesale prices on the open market without a long-term contract) to a fixed-price, long-term contracted revenue stream with an investment-grade counterparty (AWS). The AWS deal should help the company pay down its high debt over the long term.

Although the AWS deal helps mitigate some risk, a substantial portion of Talen's revenue still comes from selling electrical power and other services on wholesale markets (primarily PJM). Since the prices on the wholesale market are volatile due to weather, grid congestion, and other factors, the company's financial performance may underperform due to adverse conditions in the PJM market, despite the deal with AWS.

The company is also subject to volatility in natural gas prices and uranium fuel costs, which can adversely impact operating expenses and the profitability of its fleet. The company uses hedging to mitigate the risk of unfavorable changes in fuel pricing. However, if its risk management strategies are ineffective or counterparties fail to perform, it could adversely affect its financial results.

Lastly, Talen relies heavily on its fleet's anchor, the Susquehanna nuclear facility. If it experiences unforeseen operating failures or extended forced outages at its nuclear facility in Pennsylvania, the result will be a significant loss of revenue and cash flow. The market would likely sell off the stock in reaction.

Talen is a buy

Although Talen's growth strategy may have higher upside, its business model, regulatory, leverage risk, and potentially greater cash flow volatility compared to Vistra's may deter more conservative investors from investing. More conservative investors who want to play upon the theme of utilities powering datacenters may be more comfortable investing in Vistra because of its diversification across more markets (including ERCOT in Texas) and energy technologies (gas/nuclear/battery storage), providing a perceived buffer against the execution risk of any single platform or location. Additionally, Vistra has a stronger and more stable financial profile.

However, growth investors would likely be more interested in Talen Energy than Vistra Corp. primarily due to Talen's high-growth strategy centered on the booming AI and data center energy demand, which offers a more aggressive and potentially transformative growth narrative. Although Vistra also powers datacenters, Talen emphasizes it as a transformative growth strategy more than Vistra.

In the September 10 Investor Day Update Call, Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Mark McFarland stated:

We were the first in the IPP space to announce a data center deal with Amazon with a behind-the-meter deal in 2024. And at the same time, we sold on the campus for $650 million. We then leveraged this first-mover advantage to close on a larger and revamped front-of-the-meter deal with Amazon earlier this year. And for those that are new to the story, that is an $18 billion notional contract lasting over 17 years with options to extend even further.

The data center deal with Amazon is transformational for Talen because it fundamentally derisks, stabilizes, and grows their financial profile, achieving three critical strategic goals for a merchant power producer. I already discussed how the deal derisks the company in the risk section.

The second benefit of the deal is that it helped stabilize Talen about a year after the company emerged from bankruptcy in 2023 by securing $650 million in cash from the sale of its data center campus to AWS, which provided it with immediate, non-dilutive capital.

The third benefit is that the company pioneered a new high-growth business model by establishing the "behind-the-meter" PPA models for data centers adjacent to power plants. Consequently, the company has established itself in the industry and in investor minds as "digital infrastructure power provider," not just a traditional wholesale IPP. If you wonder why the stock receives a premium valuation over most utilities, it's because it pioneered the first BTM deal with the Amazon deal. It serves as a "proof-of-concept" for the Cumulus Growth Platform, a convergence of digital infrastructure and clean power. Talen aims to replicate its initial "behind-the-meter" PPA deal with other digital infrastructure providers, such as hyperscalers, cloud, AI, crypto, and blockchain clients, by leveraging its diverse portfolio of nuclear, gas, and renewables as energy sources. If Talen is unable to use the BTM PPA because of regulatory hurdles, it will use FTM instead. Suppose Talen can replicate its Cumulus Growth Platform success in other locations; the company's upside may exceed current estimates by both the company and analysts.

Although the market is unlikely to award the stock a PEG ratio of 1.0, and it is unlikely to rise 254% in the near future, the stock still has upside. If it achieves its goal of an adjusted FCF annual growth of 35% to 40% up until 2028, momentum investors will likely continue bidding the stock price higher. As a result, I rate Talen a buy for momentum growth investors.

data.title: Constellation Brands: Near-Term Headwinds, But Valuation Attractive With Potential Recovery Likely In The Next Fiscal Year
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) (NEOE:STZ:CA) is seeing some revenue headwinds from softer consumer sentiments, especially among its core and loyal Hispanic customer base, the trends are not worsening. The distributor inventory is also lean, and as the company laps easier comparisons in the back half, Y/Y revenue performance should become less negative, with a potential recovery likely in the next fiscal year. The company is also doing a good job in terms of innovation and shelf space gains, which positions it well to gain share during the upcycle.

Margins should also recover once volume growth resumes and the near-term impact from distributor contract resets after the recent divestitures in the wine segment subsides. The valuation is at a significant discount versus historical levels, and since trends are not getting incrementally worse, I believe longer-term investors can consider buying the stock.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Earlier this month, Constellation reported soft Q2 results with net sales down 15% Y/Y on a reported basis and 8% organically. The beer business, which accounts for the majority of the company’s revenue, saw a revenue decline of 7% Y/Y as shipments fell 8.7% Y/Y, which was partially offset by the pricing increase. The depletions were down 2.7% as the company’s customer base, especially its core and loyal Hispanic customers, continued to be worried about their personal finances due to the tough macro environment. In addition, destocking at distributor levels added to the pain.

The Wine and Spirits segment saw sales decline of 65% Y/Y due to the divestiture of low-end brands, including SVEDKA and several mainstream wine labels, last year. Organically, sales were down 19% Y/Y due to distributor contract resets following divestitures.

Moving forward, while the company's revenues should remain under pressure driven by macro challenges, especially stretched finances and weak consumer sentiment among Hispanic consumers (who are a key demographic for its beer portfolio), the company is executing well and is poised to lap some easier comparisons in the coming quarters. This should help its revenue trends become less negative in the back half of this year and aid the revenue recovery in the next fiscal year.

The company has been managing through the slowdown by focusing on what it can control and doing a good job in terms of innovation, price-pack architecture, and distribution. Innovation has been one of the biggest growth drivers for the company, accounting for roughly 30% of total growth over the last five years, adding around $3.4 bn. The 2022 launch of Corona Sunbrew has turned into a clear win, and it now ranks as the #1 new beer brand by dollar sales and the #4 share gainer across the entire beer category in this year.

The company's consumer-focused innovation strategy is the key driver behind this success. The concept for Corona Sunbrew came straight out of observing consumer behaviour, that people were mixing orange juice with Corona and drinking it during spring break. I believe this consumer-focused innovation should continue to help the company post above-market growth moving forward as well.

The company is also doing a good job in terms of price-pack architecture to soften the impact of slower volumes. The company is expanding its range across price points and pack sizes to reach consumers with different spending power. This is important right now, given the financial strain on its core demography. A good example of it is how the company tactically repositioned Modelo Oro and Premier at lower price points to keep them affordable. This shows flexibility and a good understanding of price elasticity and should help revenues until the demand picks up.

Another driver for the company's outperformance is the shelf space gains and extended retail reach, helped by over 30,000 "Shopper-first” shelf initiatives and new distribution points. This is helping the company gain share, and Constellation's Beer business gained 0.4 points of dollar share in the total beer category in Q2 FY26.

In addition to good execution, the company's sales should also benefit from easier comparisons, especially in the beer business. The company's beer segment sales in Q1 FY25 and Q2 FY25 were up 8% Y/Y and 6% Y/Y, respectively, while the growth slowed to 3% Y/Y in Q3 FY25 and was flat Y/Y in Q4 FY25. So, the comparisons are getting easier in the coming quarters. Further, last quarter, there was a good amount of distributor inventory rebalancing, and shipments were down 8.7% Y/Y versus just a 2.7% drop in depletions. So, moving forward with lean inventories at distributors and easing comps, sales should become less negative in the back half.

On the Wine & Spirits side, the 2025 divestitures and some distributor contract adjustments are creating some noise, but the underlying trends are fine. The business, now focused purely on higher-end wine and craft spirits, is actually outperforming the premium wine segment in both dollar and volume. Depletions were up ~2% in Q2 FY26, led by Kim Crawford and Mi CAMPO. Once the temporary headwinds from divestitures and contract resets roll off, growth should normalize.

Overall, while Constellation isn't immune to the macro drag, its strong execution around innovation, pricing flexibility, and shelf-space expansion, coupled with easing comparisons, lean inventory, and contract resets-related noise subsiding, should result in revenue trends being less negative in the coming quarters and should aid recovery in the next year and beyond.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

In Q2 FY26, the company reported a ~210 bps Y/Y decline in comparable operating margin, which came in at 35.7%. Margins were impacted by fixed cost deleverage due to softer volumes in the beer business, tariffs, marketing investments, and divestiture-related impact and post-transaction adjustments with distributors in the wine business.

The beer segment margin declined 200 bps Y/Y and came in at 40.6% while the wine and spirits segment operating margins flipped from +18.1% in the same quarter last year to -14.6% in Q2 FY26.

Moving forward, Constellation's margin outlook is mixed in the near term. The beer business continues to face pressure from tariffs and volume deleverage, but the company is also focused on reducing costs that should help offset some of its impact.

STZ has delivered around $105 mn in cost reductions year-to-date under its ongoing productivity and supply chain programs. These include supplies and sourcing optimization, and some logistical improvements like shifting to 60-foot rail cars and implementing double stacking to improve transport density and lower freight costs.

In addition to ongoing productivity and supply chain programs, the company also announced a new restructuring initiative in 2025. Under this program, the company intends to drive more than $200 mn in net annualized cost savings by FY28, mainly through headcount rationalization, manufacturing footprint optimization, and back-office streamlining.

I believe as the company laps tariff and volume headwinds in the next fiscal year and the benefits of cost reduction initiatives continue to show up in the results, the beer segment should revert to Y/Y margin expansion from FY27 onwards.

In the wine and spirits business, margins should recover sequentially in the second half as the second half has higher volumes seasonally. The business also sees higher contribution from direct-to-consumer and vintage releases in the back half, which carry better margins. In the medium term, as the impact of distributor contract resets post the recent divestiture fades, the margin should see substantial improvement.

Overall, I remain optimistic about the company's margins, especially from the next financial year.

Valuation and Rating

STZ is trading at roughly 12.3x FY26 consensus EPS of $11.51 and about 11.37x FY27 EPS of $12.45, which is a meaningful discount to its five-year average forward P/E of around 19.85x. That’s a pretty big gap for a company with brands as strong as Constellation’s, and it feels like the market is pricing in a prolonged slump that’s unlikely to last. I don't think this is justified.

The current headwinds are more cyclical than structural. The macros are tough right now, no question, but they’re not getting sequentially worse. Consumer spending among core demographics is stretched, but the beer category has stabilized a bit, inventories are clean, and comps start to ease in the back half. That combination alone should make the reported numbers look better in 2H FY26. If macros start to recover next year, even modestly, that should result in good revenue growth. At the same time, the ongoing cost-savings initiatives, logistics efficiencies, and restructuring benefits should provide a steady lift to margins over the next couple of years.

This is one of those setups where patience can pay off. The valuation already reflects a lot of bad news, and the company is doing everything it can control, like executing on innovation, managing pricing architecture, and keeping costs tight. Once the macro backdrop turns, and it always does, Constellation is positioned to benefit from both earnings growth and a likely re-rating closer to its historical multiple range. A move back even halfway toward its average multiple would imply solid upside from the current levels.

The dividend yield of about 2.9% should also provide downside support while waiting for sentiment to turn.

Overall, I think the worst is behind the stock. Trends are starting to look less negative, comps are easing, and the underlying execution remains strong. At these levels, STZ offers an attractive risk-reward for investors willing to hold through the next 12-18 months. I’d rate it a Buy, with earnings recovery as well as multiple expansion driving upside as conditions normalize.

Risks

The consumption trends among Hispanic Consumers are tough but not getting incrementally worse. I am expecting this stabilization to continue. However, if there is another leg down in the consumption patterns, the FY27 recovery that I am betting on might not materialize.

In the long run, one risk to watch closely is the gradual rise in cannabis adoption as its legalization expands and social acceptance grows. If cannabis competes with beer and other alcoholic categories, the company's business might be adversely impacted.

data.title: Matador Resources: Accelerates Development Activity In Late 2025
data.description:
Matador Resources (NYSE:MTDR) announced strong Q3 2025 production results that were driven by a combination of well outperformance and a higher than expected number of wells being turned to sales.

Matador has also accelerated its 2025 development activity, resulting in a roughly $250 million capex increase at guidance midpoint. This is expected to have a modest amount (1% to 2%) of impact on its 2025 production, but will have more impact on Matador's 2026 production (which is around 5% to 6% more than what I previously modeled).

Matador is now projected to generate approximately $700 million in 2026 free cash flow at $60 WTI oil, which is around 3.8x dividend coverage with its increased $0.375 per share quarterly dividend.

I have trimmed Matador's estimated value by $1 to $68 per share due to its slightly increased risk (with the extra spending in 2025). The extra spending will be partially recovered from the additional production in 2026.

Dividend Increase

Matador increased its quarterly dividend by 20%, from $0.3125 per share to $0.375 per share. It is now paying $1.50 per share in dividends per year (or approximately $186 million in total). This is the seventh dividend increase since Matador initiated a $0.025 per share quarterly dividend in 2021.

Matador's dividend coverage looks healthy, though, as I estimate it can generate roughly $700 million in 2026 free cash flow at $60 WTI oil.

Q3 2025 Results

Matador's Q3 2025 production was strong at 209,184 BOEPD, which was 5% above the midpoint of its guidance for the quarter. Matador's oil production was 119,556 barrels per day, which was 2% above the midpoint of its guidance.

Close to 30% of the total production beat was due to the natural gas production results from six non-operated Haynesville wells. As well, Matador mentioned that it turned 34.5 net operated wells to sales in Q3 2025 compared to original plans for 30 net operated wells. Matador also noted that its well performance has been strong.

Matador's Q3 2025 D/C/E capex was $430 million, around $95 million more than its guidance midpoint. Matador mentioned that $15 million of the increase was due to the additional 4.5 net operated wells turned to sales, another $15 million due to higher than anticipated non-operated well activity, and $9 million due to adding working interest to the wells turned to sale during the quarter. The remaining $56 million was due to some development activity (for wells scheduled to be turned to sales in Q4) being moved into Q3.

The increased spending reduced Matador's free cash flow to $93 million in Q3 2025. It also mentioned repurchasing 1.3 million shares for $55 million (at an average price of approximately $41 per share).

Guidance Changes

Matador increased its full-year 2025 total production guidance by 2% and its 2025 oil production guidance by 1%. This was due to Matador's relatively strong results in Q3 2025 along with accelerated operating activities (resulting in 12 additional net wells being completed in 2025 compared to earlier guidance).

Matador's D&C capex guidance for 2025 has increased by $235 million at guidance midpoint, while its midstream capex guidance has increased by $15 million.

Matador noted that its well costs (per lateral foot) have been reduced by approximately 4%, but its overall capex budget is increased due to the additional completions in 2025 as well as a higher than anticipated amount of non-operated well activity.

As well, Matador mentioned that it expects to turn 13.6 net operated wells to sales in January 2026, with the associated increased late 2025 spending thus helping to boost production in 2026.

2026 Outlook

Matador mentioned expectations for 210,000 BOEPD in total production during 2026 (including approximately 123,750 barrels per day of oil production). This is around 5% higher total production and 6% higher oil production than what I had modeled for Matador before, albeit with a larger capex budget (both in late 2025 and 2026).

The current strip for 2026 involves approximately $60 WTI oil and $4.00 NYMEX natural gas now. At those commodity prices, I project Matador to generate $3.462 billion in revenues after hedges.
